Questions.Title: Escalation – Explicit Customer Request
Questions.Text: A customer sends: "This is frustrating. I have explained my issue twice and nothing is being resolved. I want to talk to a real person NOW." The agent has all customer account information and conversation history. The agent cannot resolve the specific issue anyway. What should the agent do?

Options.A: Acknowledge the frustration and ask one targeted question to understand the specific issue before escalating
Options.B: Immediately call `escalate_to_human` with the conversation history
Options.C: First call `get_customer` and `lookup_order` to gather account context, then escalate to a human agent
Options.D: Briefly explain what the agent can help with and offer to resolve the issue quickly

Questions.Explanation: Customer explicitly demanded a human NOW, agent cannot resolve the issue, and agent already has all context. Escalate immediately with conversation history so the human agent has full context.

Questions.Title: Mid-Process Escalation
Questions.Text: Your agent is handling a billing dispute. After calling `get_customer` and `lookup_order`, it identifies the dispute involves a promotional pricing error requiring manager approval — beyond the agent authorization level. How should the workflow handle this mid-process escalation?

Options.A: Compile a structured handoff (customer details + order info + identified issue) then call `escalate_to_human` with a reference ID
Options.B: Persist the complete conversation and tool response history to a database, then call `escalate_to_human`
Options.C: Attempt `process_refund` anyway, escalating only if the refund fails
Options.D: Call `escalate_to_human` passing only the customer original message

Questions.Explanation: Preserve all gathered intelligence in a structured handoff. Human agent receives complete context: customer details, order info, root cause, and reference ID — no customer re-explanation needed.

Questions.Title: Agentic Loop – Stop Reason
Questions.Text: A customer contacts the agent about a warranty claim on a power drill. Resolving this requires multiple sequential tool calls: `get_customer`, `get_product`, and then either `process_refund` or `unable_to_honor`. What is the primary mechanism your application uses to determine whether to continue the loop or stop?

Options.A: You check the `stop_reason` field in each API response — the loop continues while it equals "tool_use" and stops when it changes to "end_turn" or another terminal value
Options.B: You manually set the `tool_choice` parameter to "none" after the final expected tool call to force Claude to stop requesting tools
Options.C: You track the number of tool calls made and exit the loop once a predefined maximum is reached
Options.D: You check whether Claude response contains a text content block — if text is present, the agent has produced its final answer

Questions.Explanation: stop_reason is the official API mechanism: stop_reason = "tool_use" → execute tool and continue; stop_reason = "end_turn" → stop loop and return final response.

Questions.Title: Escalation Triggers
Questions.Text: You are implementing escalation logic for when the agent should call `escalate_to_human`. Which approach most reliably identifies cases that genuinely require human intervention?

Options.A: Instruct the agent to escalate when: the customer requests a human, the issue requires policy exceptions, or the agent cannot make progress toward resolution
Options.B: Implement sentiment analysis that monitors for frustration indicators (negative language, exclamation marks) and triggers escalation when reaching a threshold
Options.C: Build a rules engine that maps specific issue types, customer segments, and product categories to escalation decisions
Options.D: Configure the agent to escalate after three consecutive tool calls that fail to resolve the customer issue

Questions.Explanation: Semantic escalation triggers describe actual situations where human intervention adds value: customer wants a human, policy exception needed, or no progress possible.

Questions.Title: Context Management – Verbose Tool Output
Questions.Text: Your agent has called `lookup_order` multiple times while investigating a customer return requests. Each response includes 40+ fields, making tool outputs represent the majority of the conversation context. The customer mentions two more orders they want to discuss. What is the most efficient way to avoid consuming additional context for future lookups?

Options.A: Move all tool responses to a vector database with semantic indexing, retrieving relevant portions as the conversation continues
Options.B: Proceed with additional lookups without modifying the existing tool output content
Options.C: Have the model generate a natural language summary of each order key details to replace the existing tool output context
Options.D: Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, return status) and replace the full tool output with just those fields

Questions.Explanation: For return requests, only 5-6 fields matter out of 40+. Filter tool output to return-relevant fields at extraction time — context consumption drops dramatically per lookup.

Questions.Title: Multi-Turn Memory
Questions.Text: The agent verifies customer identity through a multi-step process before resetting passwords. During testing, after the customer answers security questions, the agent asks them to provide their name again, as if the earlier exchange never happened. What is the most likely cause?

Options.A: The conversation history is not being passed in subsequent API requests
Options.B: The prompt lacks instructions telling Claude to remember information across multiple exchanges
Options.C: The verification tool is clearing the agent internal state after each successful validation step
Options.D: Claude memory retention is limited to two conversation turns by default, requiring explicit configuration to extend

Questions.Explanation: Claude has zero persistent memory between API calls. If the application fails to include prior messages in the API request, Claude starts fresh every call — the most common multi-turn implementation bug.
